coredata.dc:title: A dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent biosensor for ultrasensitive detection of glibenclamide using S-CDs/CdS quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Gazizadeh, Masoud
Dehghan, Gholamreza
Soleymani, Jafar
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, sulfide-doped carbon dots (S-CDs)/cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS QDs) ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor has been developed for sensitive and selective determination of glibenclamide (GLC) in biological fluids. The method was based on the quenching effect of GLC on the dual-emission intensity of the S-CDs/CdS QDs system at 420 nm and 650 nm, which are related to S-CDs and CdS QDs, respectively. The fluorimetric data analysis indicated that the fluorescence signals of the system were quenched by adding GLC in a concentration-dependent manner. A good linear relationship was observed between GLC concentration and the quenched fluorescence intensity of the S-CDs/CdS QDs in the range of 0.3 nM−10.0 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) value was estimated to be 0.12 nM. Furthermore, under optimum conditions, GLC was detected in spiked human serum sample (as real media) using the developed ratiometric nanosensor with an accuracy of 99.6%. According to the results, the developed dual-emission system can be used as a reliable method for the quantitative detection of GLC in biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Diabetes mellitus
Sulfide-doped carbon dots
Cadmium sulfide quantum dots
Glibenclamide
Ratiometric fluorescence

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorometric assay of GLC by S-CDs/CdS QDs
Selectivity of S-CDs/CdS QDs for GLC
Fluorometric detection of GLC in plasma samples
Quenching mechanism and GLC/CdS binding kinetic
Analytical features of the developed method
Determination of GLC in plasma samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the University of Tabriz and the Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran. It is declared that all ethical issues were regarded during working with biological samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CdS QDs (A), S-CDs (B), and ATR-FTIR spectra of CdS QDs, S-CDs, S-CDs/CdS QDs, and S-CDs/CdS QDs in the presence of GLC (C), and excitation and emission spectra of S-CDs (D), and excitation and emission) spectra of CdS QDs (E).
FESEM images and DLS of S-CDs/CdS QDs in the presence of GLC (A) and (B), UV–Vis absorption spectra of CdS QDs, S-CDs, S-CDs/CdS QDs, and S-CDs/CdS QDs in the presence of GLC (C), and buffer pH (D).

The effect of GLC on the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs/CdS QDs and the linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching ratio of S-CDs/CdS QDs and the logarithm of GLC concentration in acetate buffer solution (0.01 M), pH = 7.5 and 25 °C (A) and the response of S-CDs/CdS QDs towards GLC detection in human plasma samples in acetate buffer solution (0.01 M), pH = 7.5 and 25 °C (B).
Design for the determination of GLC based on its quenching effect on the fluorescence emission of S-CDs and CdS QDs.
Comparison between some previously reported methods for the detection of GLC and the proposed system.
Precision, accuracy, and recoveries for the determination of GLC in human plasma samples.
Details of the stability of the proposed method in the plasma sample.
Effect of some common interfering species on the detection of GLC (70 nM).
Determination of GLC in spiked plasma samples.

head.ce:title: A dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent biosensor for ultrasensitive detection of glibenclamide using S-CDs/CdS quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Faculty of Natural Sciences, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biology, Faculty of Natural Sciences, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, sulfide-doped carbon dots (S-CDs)/cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS QDs) ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor has been developed for sensitive and selective determination of glibenclamide (GLC) in biological fluids. The method was based on the quenching effect of GLC on the dual-emission intensity of the S-CDs/CdS QDs system at 420 nm and 650 nm, which are related to S-CDs and CdS QDs, respectively. The fluorimetric data analysis indicated that the fluorescence signals of the system were quenched by adding GLC in a concentration-dependent manner. A good linear relationship was observed between GLC concentration and the quenched fluorescence intensity of the S-CDs/CdS QDs in the range of 0.3 nM−10.0 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) value was estimated to be 0.12 nM. Furthermore, under optimum conditions, GLC was detected in spiked human serum sample (as real media) using the developed ratiometric nanosensor with an accuracy of 99.6%. According to the results, the developed dual-emission system can be used as a reliable method for the quantitative detection of GLC in biological samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glibenclamide (GLC; Fig. 1 ), also known as glyburide, belongs to the sulfonylurea category and its clinical utilization dates back to the 1960s [1,2]. It is commonly used for the treatment of type II diabetes mellitus and reduces blood glucose by inhibiting ATP-sensitive potassium channels in pancreatic beta cells [3–5]. Up to now, different analytical methods have been reported for the determination of GLC in pharmaceutical formulations and biological media including spectrofluorimetric methods, [6], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7,8], capillary gas chromatography [9], high-performance thin-layer chromatography (HPTLC) and electroanalytical methods [10]. Despite their efficiency, most of these approaches have limitations such as high costs, time-consuming experimental procedures, and the use of hazardous chemical reagents. In spiring the problems, researchers are motivated to develop new simple, selective, more powerful, and inexpensive biodevices for the determination of GLC in pharmaceutical formulations and biological fluids.

In the past years, nanotechnology has attracted widespread attention in different areas, such as medicine, biology, engineering, electronics, catalysis, optoelectronics, the textile industry, physics, chemistry, and so on [11–14]. Nanostructured materials are able to bridge the gap between the bulk and molecular levels [15]. Fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and carbon quantum dots are carbon-based nanomaterials that have received considerable attention in biological applications such as sensing, drug transport, imaging, and therapy [16,17].

The nanomaterial-based optical sensing method has shown significant promise as a detection tool due to its rapid and easy monitoring of fluorescence changes generated by sensor-analyte interactions. Due to their precise electrical and optical properties, quantum dots (QDs), a new class of fluorescent particles have emerged as a promising candidate for a variety of applications in recent years [16,18–21]. QDs are nanostructured semiconductors, with nanometer-sized diameters that exhibit discrete energy levels, and their bandgap is proportional to their size and is composed of groups II-VI, III-V, or IV elements [11,18]. The size and composition of QDs give them unique luminescence characteristics and distinctive optoelectronic properties such as broad absorption and narrow and tunable emission spectra [22–25]. Different kinds of metal QDs such as CdS, CdTe, CdSe, PbSe, PbS, HgTe, Sb2S3, InP, Ag2S, and so on, have been used in many sciences like chemistry, physics, and pharmaceutical sciences, the main reasons for which are related to the optical properties, the way light is transmitted, and their excellent detection. Carbon-based QDs play an important role in nanotechnology due to their tunable size properties, biocompatible nature, and high surface-to-volume ratio. These nanostructures have unique physicochemical, electrical, and optical properties that have found wide applications in industry, imaging, detection, drug delivery, and medicine [14,24].

In the present study, a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor using sulfide-doped carbon dots (S-CDs)/cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS QDs) has been developed for the detection of GLC (Scheme 1 ). To develop a ratiometric sensor, we should regard two main features, 1) to acquire two separated peaks from two or one compound, and 2) the analyte should mainly affect one or in some cases both of the nanoparticle emissions. To reach these perquisites, the functional groups of both probe and the analyte are important. In this case, it was hypothesized that the non-bonding electron pairs of the functional groups of the analyte may form a dative bond with the vacant orbital of the Cd ions. In addition, the interaction between the thiol groups of the GLC and the CdS particles may have resulted in changes in the fluorescence of the CdS. Various techniques were used to characterize the synthesized particles, including Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. S-CDs/CdS QDs were used as a new and sensitive nanoprobe for the determination of GLC in test tubes and real samples. The S-CDs/CdS QDs emitted dual emission fluorescence when excited, one from the S-CDs and the other from the CdS QDs and their fluorescence was sensitivity quenched in response to GLC.

Cadmium chloride monohydrate (CdCl2·H2O), ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), acetone, propanol (PrOH), acetonitrile (ACN), chloroform, sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), aerosol-OT or sodium bis(2-Ethylhexyl) (AOT), Brij, Triton X-100 and Tween20 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). Citric acid, mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) were provided from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). GLC was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). All materials were of analytical grade and were utilized without any purification steps. Also, all standard solutions were prepared from standard materials using double distilled water.

Firstly, 1.0 g of citric acid and 1.5 mL of MPA were dissolved in 50.0 mL of deionized water then the prepared mixture was transferred into the synthesis container, which is made of resistant Teflon, and the lid was completely closed to prevent the material from evaporating, and in the next step, the synthesis container was placed inside the steel chamber and heated in an oven for 8 h at 180 °C. After cooling at room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm to remove the insoluble materials. Then, the supernatant containing S-CDs was filtered by a 0.22 μm micropore membrane filter. Subsequently, the prepared solution containing S-CDs was dialyzed for 72 h in a dialysis bag with a 1,000 Da molecular weight cut-off.

A common hydrothermal method was used for the preparation of CdS QDs by the following approach: in a typical process, 101 mg of CdCl2·H2O was dissolved in 95 mL of deionized water. Then, 85 µL of MPA was added to the prepared solution, and the pH level of the solution (solution A) was adjusted to 11 using NaOH (0.1 M). Subsequently, a 0.2 M citric acid solution was prepared, then added to solution A and heated at 100 °C. For further purification to remove unreacted materials, the prepared final solution was centrifuged at 4 °C for 15 min at 10,000 rpm and then dialyzed for about 24 h.

The morphology of the prepared particles was studied by a FEG-SEM MIRA3 TESCAN microscope (Brno, Czech Republic). The attenuated total reflection Fourier-transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra were recorded on a BRUKER, Tensor 27 instrument for the investigation of the functional groups of S-CDs, CdS, S-CDs/CdS QDs, and S-CDs/CdS QDs/GBC in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. All fluorescence spectra were recorded on a FP-750 Jasco spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) in 10 mm quartz microcells, at room temperature. Also, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a UV spectrophotometer (T60, PG Instruments ltd, Leicestershire, UK). A laser-scattering technique (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK) was performed to analyze the mean diameter and zeta-potential (ZP) of the synthesized particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric assay of GLC by S-CDs/CdS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The S-CDs/CdS QDs-based fluorescent system with dual-emission bands was applied for the fluorometric detection of GLC. Typically, a fixed concentration of S-CDs/CdS QDs (2000 ppm, 800 ppm) was transferred into the cuvette containing 0.5 mL buffer. After recording the signal of the ratiometric probe, ranges concentrations of GLC (0.3 nM-10.0 μM) were separately introduced into the S-CDs/CdS QDs probe and incubated for 10 min. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of the prepared samples was recorded at 420 nm (λem (S-CDs) = 420 nm) and 650 nm (λem (CdS) = 650 nm) by exciting at 370 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of S-CDs/CdS QDs for GLC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the selectivity of the prepared ratiometric nanoprobe towards GLC, the response of the nanosensor to some interfering substances such as ions Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl-, Cu2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+, pharmaceuticals of naproxen, mesalazine, erythromycin, amlodipine, penicillin, cephalexin, acetaminophen, ceftriaxone, amoxicillin, aspirin, diclofenac, cetirizine, biomaterials of starch, lactose, uric acid, sucrose, and vitamins of B1, B2, E, D3, and C were tested in the presence of GLC under the optimal conditions. In this case, 120.0 µL of interfering substances were added to 3.0 mL of S-CDs/CdS QDs suspension containing 70.0 nM GLC, and the emission intensities of the samples were recorded at 420 nm and 650 nm under excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric detection of GLC in plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the reliability of the designed nanosensor for the detection of GLC, human plasma samples were used as biological samples. For this purpose, human plasma samples of healthy volunteers were provided by the Iranian Blood Transfusion Organization (IBTO, licensecode: IR.TBZMED.VCR.REC.1400.420) and stored in the freezer at −20 °C. Before further analysis, the prepared sample was thawed gently and spiked with various concentrations of the standard solution of GLC (1 nM to 10 μM). Then, deproteinization of the prepared samples was performed by adding 1.5 mL of acetonitrile. After mixing for 5.0 min, the prepared solutions were centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 10.0 min. The precipitate was removed and the supernatant was collected for further analysis.

The morphology of the fabricated S-CDs QDs and CdS was evaluated using TEM analysis. Fig. 2 displays TEM images of the synthesized CdS (A) and S-CDs (B). The obtained images showed that the synthesized nanomaterials have a spherical structure and approximately the same size as their exact sizes using DLS analysis 5–8 nm was reported to be in close agreement with TEM data and also these nanomaterials have a smooth surface The functional groups of the surface of the prepared particles were evaluated based on the ATR-FTIR spectroscopy analysis. The recorded ATR-FTIR spectra of CdS, S-CDs, S-CDs/CdS QDs and S-CDs/CdS QDs in the presence of GLC are shown in Fig. 2C. Both S-CDs and CdS QDs showed different functional peaks. The observed peaks at 1650 and 1573 cm−1 correspond to the CO vibration and the NH bending vibration, respectively. The weak peak at 723 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the CS bond and the peaks at 3394 and 2946 cm−1 are related to the contraction vibration of OH and CH, respectively [25–27]. This figure indicates that CdS QDs and S-CDs were successfully synthesized. The ATR-FTIR spectrum of S-CDs/CdS QDs was also recorded and the results showed that the general shape of the S-CDs/CdS QDs is a mixed form of S-CDs and CdS QDs, confirming the successful synthesis of S-CDs/CdS QDs. Furthermore, Fig. 2C shows the ATR-FTIR spectrum of S-CDs/CdS QDs in the presence of GLC. According to this figure, the observed alterations in the intensities of bands and the shifting of bands confirm the interaction of GLB with the S-CDs/CdS QDs system. Finally, considering that the basis of the work is analytical, the excitation and emission spectra of S-CDs (Fig. 2D) and CdS QDs (Fig. 2E) were also recorded. ZP analysis was performed to investigate the surface charge and stability of synthesized particles (Fig. 1SA). The obtained ZP of the particles was found to be 0.690 mV. Also, the particle size of materials is normally observed by DLS (Fig. 1SB).

The QY of the S-CDs was determined by comparing the emission of the nanomaterial with a reference material with known QY. In this work, tryptophan was selected as a reference and its QY value has been reported as 13 %. The following equation was utilized to calculate the QY of the S-CDs [28,29].
Q Y
S - C D s
=
Q Y
TrP
A
Trp
A
S - C D s
F
S - C D s
F
Trp
η
S - C D s
η
Trp
2

Where A and η are the absorbance and the refractive index value of the media which in aqueous media equals to be 1. The calculated value for QY of S-CDs is 16.5 % which is close to the reported values [28,29].

According to DLS results, the quantum dots used in this study have dimensions of 2.0 to 8.0 nm. Therefore, their solution can show considerable dispersion. The intensity of the scattering depends on the size of the particles, so larger particles show more intense scattering values. In order to investigate the possibility of particle accumulation in the presence of GLC, different amounts of GLC (0.001, 0.07, 0.1, 0.5, 1, and 10 μM) were added to the solution of quantum dots and their scattering spectra were recorded. The results showed that the higher the amount of GLC, the stronger the dispersion of the solution (Fig. 1SC). This indicates that as GLC molecules attach to the surface of the quantum dots, causing the particles clump together, thus changing the size of the quantum dots [30,31]. The FESEM images and DLS analysis of S-CDs/CdS QDs upon interaction with GLC are shown in (Fig. 3 A and B). The obtained results indicated that the interaction of S-CDs/CdS QDs with GLC resulted in the aggregation of some of the particles.

To confirm the S-CDs/CdS QDs synthesis, spectroscopic studies were performed by recording the absorption spectra of CdS, S-CDs, and S-CDs/CdS QDs in the range of 200 to 800 nm. As can be seen in Fig. 3C, S-CDs QDs and CdS QDs show a unique absorption peak at 280 and 515 nm, respectively [32–34]. Furthermore, the absorption spectrum of S-CDs/CdS QDs showed two characteristic peaks at 485 nm (related to the
π →
π *
transition of electrons of CC) and 412 nm (correspond to the
n →
π *
transition of electrons of CO), which indicates the blue and red shift in the absorption edge of CdS QDs and S-CDs QDs [35,36]. In addition, the absorption spectrum of S-CDs/CdS QDs was evaluated in the presence of 1 µM GLC. The recorded data showed that the GLC can quench the absorption spectrum of S-CDs/CdS QDs (Fig. 3C). No considerable overlap was observed between the UV absorption of GLC and S-CDs/CdS QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism and GLC/CdS binding kinetic
ce:section.ce:para:
There are some mechanisms for quenching the fluorescence emission of the nanoparticles i.e. dynamic and static quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), etc. Because the analyte mainly changes the fluorescence intensity of the CdS QDs, we performed all calculations at 650 nm. There is a spectral overlap between the excitation absorption spectra of the S-CDs/CdS QDs and GLC which suggests the FRET and IFE quenching mechanism. The pKa value of GLC is 5.3 which is in somewhat negative charges at the optimum pH of 7.5. The ZP value of S-CDs/CdS QDs is + 0.69 mV. Therefore, there is no powerful electrostatic interaction between the quencher and S-CDs/CdS QDs as a long-distance interaction, rejecting the FRET mechanism. Upon addition of the GLC to the S-CDs/CdS QDs, there was observed new absorption peak, proposing dynamic quenching of mechanism [37]. This hypothesis has been reinforced by the fact that the slope (KSV) of the stern-Volmer equation was increased upon increasing the temperature (Fig. 1SD). The Stern-Volmer equation could be symbolized as [38]:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
sv
Q

where F0, F, and [Q] denote the fluorescence emission of S-CDs/CdS QDs in the absence and presence of GLC, respectively. KSV is defined as the Stern-Volmer constant. Since the KSV at the various temperature is increased upon rising the temperature, the dynamic quenching mechanism could be proposed for the GLC and the S-CDs/CdS QDs interactions as the most possible quenching mechanism. The GLC binding constant to the S-CDs/CdS QDs could be determined by the below equation [28]:
log
F 0
- F
F
= l o g
K b
+ n l o g
Q

Where n and Kb represent the binding site number and binding affinity constants which are calculated by correlation log (F0-F/F) against log [GLC]. The calculated value of Kb is 0.157 × 10 4 M−1, confirming the presence of the binding sites between GLC and the S-CDs/CdS QDs. The n value was calculated as 0.5, which means that one S-CDs/CdS QDs could be attached to at least one quencher molecule. This phenomenon doubly approved the quenching mechanism of the GLC on the S-CDs/CdS QDs.

To achieve an excellent response of the prepared nanosensor for the detection of GLC, different parameters such as pH, temperature, the type of buffers, solvent and surfactant, interaction time, and the ratio of fluorophores (CdS QDs/S-CDs) were optimized. For this purpose, the fluorescence ratio change (F650/F420) was used as an analytical response, and all the experiments were carried out in the presence of 1.0 μM of GLC. The stability of the nanosensor has also been checked for ultraviolet (UV) and salt effects on the response of the sensor.

The buffering effect on the prepared nanosensor fluorescent ratio was assessed using three different types of buffers such as sodium acetate, Tris-HCl, and phosphate buffers. The S-CDs /CdS QDs showed two main emission bands at 420 nm and 650 nm, which are related to S-CDs and CdS, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2SA, a minimum fluorescence ratio was obtained in the presence of an acetate buffer solution. So, acetate buffer was selected as the best buffer solution for experimental investigations. Fig. 2SB shows the effect of different concentrations of acetate buffer solution (10, 20, 50, 100, and 200 mM) on the response of the prepared ratiometric nanosensor. It has been reported that the concentration of buffer has a considerable effect on the QDs emission intensity and the reaction of QDs with different ligands [39]. The results indicated that the minimum fluorescence ratio (F650/F420) of the system was achieved when the buffer concentration was 100.0 mM. In addition, the effect of different pH values (ranging from 2.5 to 11.5) on the fluorescence quenching of the prepared nanosensor was investigated. The obtained results indicated that the F650/F420 value increased by moving to more acidic and basic pHs (Fig. 3D). The functional group on the S-CDs/CdS QDs system can be protonated and de-protonated in acidic and basic media and the concentrations of H+ and OH− can modify the surface of the S-CDs/CdS QDs. Regarding the hydrolysis of the Cd+2 at the low and high pHs, a competition between the H+ and Cd2+ to interact with the GLC may be taking place [40,41]. Therefore, the response of the system was decreased at acidic and basic media and the maximum fluorescence quenching of the ratiometric sensor in the presence of GLC was obtained at pH 7.5, which was selected as the optimum value.

The fluorescence quenching of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was investigated during the time (0 to 40 min) and the results confirmed that the maximum emission quenching of the system was obtained after 15 min incubation in the presence of GLC (Fig. 3SA).

Furthermore, the effects of temperature, ultraviolet (UV) irradiation time, and slat concentration were optimized. As described in Fig. 3SA to D, the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs/CdS QDs remains stableat different temperatures. However, 25 °C was selected as the best temperature for experimental analysis. Moreover, continuous UV irradiation for 360 min caused a slight change in the maximum fluorescence ratio (Fig. 3SC). These results indicated the good anti-photobleaching capability of the ratiometric nanosensor [42]. Ionic strength is one of the most important parameters, affecting the signal of nanosensor in any system [43]. In this study, to regulate the ionic strength,NaCl (with various concentrations of 0 to 250 mM) was employed and its effect on the fluorescence quenching of the system was checked. Studies have shown that the salt concentrations used for the suggested nanosensor system have little effect on system performance (less than 3 %) (Fig. 3SD).

The effect of the volumetric ratio of CdS QDs to S-CDs on the response of the system was examined. Data obtained showed that maximum fluorescence quenching of the nanosensor was observed at 800 ppm CdS QDs and 2000 ppm of S-CDs in the presence and absence of GLC (Fig. 3SE). Under the optimum conditions, the response of the developed nanosensor in the presence of different types of solvents such as water, MeOH, chloroform, PrOH, EtOH, acetone, and ACN was evaluated and the results indicated that the maximum response of the system was achieved by the use of ACN (Fig. 4SA). Also, the fluorescence quenching of the ratiometric nanosensor was the highest amount when the concentration of ACN in the reaction mixture was 500 µL (Fig. 4SB).

In addition, the effect of various types of surfactants such as CTAB, SDS, AOT, X100, Tween 20, and Brij on the decreased fluorescence intensity of the developed nanosensor was checked. From the results shown in (Fig. 4SC and D), it was found that at concentrations of the critical micelle concentration (CMC) and concentrations below the CMC, the quenched fluorescence intensity of the S-CDs/CdS QDs was the highest by the use of CTAB and Brij, respectively (without and with GLC).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical features of the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: It was found that GLC caused a considerable quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of the S-CDs/CdS QDs system. So, the developed dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor was performed for the detection of GLC. For this purpose, the relationship between the emission intensity of the system and the concentrations of GLC under optimal conditions was evaluated in the presence of various concentrations of GLC (0.3 nM to 10.0 µM) by recording the emission intensity of the system at 420 nm and 650 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the nanosensor was gradually quenched with increasing concentrations of GLC, confirming the good sensitivity of the S-CDs/CdS QDs nanosensor towards GLC (Fig. 4 A). Also, as shown in Fig. 4A, a good linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching ratios (F650/F420) and the logarithmic concentration of GLC was obtained with the linear regression equation of y = -0.3551x-0.6995 (R2 = 0.9973). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.12 nM. To investigate the applicability of the proposed dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor, the determination of GLC was performed under the optimum conditions in the human serum samples based on the described procedure. The obtained results indicated that by increasing GLC concentration the fluorescence intensity was decreased, which was proportional to the GLC concentration. Also, a linear correlation was observed between the fluorescence ratio (F650/F420) concentration of GLC in the range of 1.0 nM to 10.0 μM with a good R2 = 0.9997 (Fig. 4B). The high sensitivity of the developed sensor may be related to its high specific surface area, which can enrich a trace amount of analyte molecules. The comparison of the S-CDs/CdS QDs nanosensor and some other reported methods for the detection of GLC has been summarized in Table 1 . This table indicates that the proposed method has a lower detection limit than previously reported methods. The developed dual-emission systems can be applied as a simple, cheap, rapid, selective, and sensitive quantitative method for the detection of GLC.

To evaluate the intra and interday accuracy and precision of the developed dual-emission ratiometric nanosensor, plasma samples were spiked with four different concentrations of GLC (Based on FDA references, the concentrations were randomly selected in the range of the lowest, average, and highest concentrations in the calibration chart). Then, the response of the system was recorded at three different times on the same day (intraday accuracy) and three different days (interday accuracy). The accuracy of the method was studied by comparison of the amounts found versus the amounts added, which provides the recovery of the method. The obtained results (Table 2 ) confirmed the satisfactory accuracy, precision, and reliabilityof the prepared nanosensor for the analysis of GLC in real samples.

The stability of the GLC in plasma samples was assessed by analyzing the samples at different concentrations of GLC (according to FDA sources) during the sample and storage procedure. After 12 h of exposure to room temperature, the plasma samples were tested for short-term stability. Also, the freeze/thaw stability was determined after three freeze/thaw cycles at −20 °C. All stability studies yielded quantities that were compared to freshly prepared samples. The precision for freeze/thaw samples ranged from 2.0 to 8.0 %, confirming that the GLC was stable in plasma for three cycles when stored at −20 °C. Also, short-term temperature showed satisfactory precision. These results (Table 3 ) indicated the stability of the developed system under the experimental conditions of the analytical runs.

To investigate the selectivity of the prepared nanosensor the possible effect of some coexisting interfering species in the real samples on the response of the system was assessed under the optimized conditions in the presence of 70.0 nM of GLC. These factors include ions such as Na+/K+ (main role in cell membrane function in the charge balance between the two sides of the membrane as a sodium/potassium pump, etc.). Mg2+ (Role in ATP activation and stability of polyphosphate compounds in DNA and RNA synthesis). Zn2+ (role in wound healing, immune system function, and activation of some enzymes, etc.). Ca2+ (role in bone strength and blood pressure regulation, etc.). Cl− (In the nervous system, the function of glycine and GABA is related to the level of chlorine ions in specific neurons. In addition, chlorine ion plays a role in maintaining the acid-base balance of the blood, which is responsible for regulating its level by the kidneys). Cu2+ (main inscription to the production of respiratory enzyme cytochrome oxidase C and superoxide dismutase, etc.). And some drugs that can be prescribed and used simultaneously with GLC and other metabolites and amino acids, the names and results of their effect on the system are presented (Table 4 ).

According to the data obtained the tolerance limits (the concentration ratio of interfering species or analyte that produces a relative error of less than 5 % in sensitivity of the method) were calculated therefore the developed dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor is able to detect GLC with a good selectivity in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GLC in plasma samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the applicability of the S-CDs/CdS QDs based fluorescent sensor for the determination of GLC, the developed system was applied for the analysis of GLC in spiked human plasma samples. Validation experiments were also done by spiking certain concentrations of the GLC standard into real samples, before any preparation process. Finally, GLC concentrations were quantified following the general procedure described in the experimental section. The results are shown in Table 5 , considering the good and acceptable recovery percentages in the selected concentrations in the calibration range, it can be confirmed that the designed probe has a good interaction with the GLC drug and is suitable for its determination.

In summary, a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor using S-CDs/CdS QDs (with two emission peaks at 650 nm and 420 nm) was developed for the specific and sensitive quantification of GLC in plasma samples. The as-synthesized particles were characterized using different methods. FESEM, ATR-FTIR, and DLS/Zeta analysis confirmed the morphology, surface, functional group, size, and surface charge of the prepared particles. The emission signals of the S-CDs/CdS QDs were quenched upon interaction with GLC in a linear manner. A good linear correlation between the fluorescence quenching of the system and the logarithmic concentration of GLC in a linear range of 0.3 nM to10.0 µM with a LOD value of 0.12 nM was observed. The selectivity of the probe was analyzed using common interfering biomolecules and ions. Also, the practicality and applicability of the prepared nanosensor were analyzed in spiked human serum samples with an appreciable recovery percentage. The developed nanosensor showed good stability, sensitivity, selectivity, and reproducibility for the determination of GLC. Regarding all beneficial features, the developed method could be utilized for the analysis of GLC in the real sample in the clinic. The advantageous function of the probe was mainly adopted by the automatic basic of the sensors which decrease the interfering effects of the co-existing materials in plasma.

Masoud Gazizadeh: Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Gholamreza Dehghan: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Jafar Soleymani: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the University of Tabriz and the Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center of Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran. It is declared that all ethical issues were regarded during working with biological samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122714.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
